stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing an 8-year-old non-Aboriginal boy with a one-week history of polyuria, polydipsia and three days of vomiting. He has lost 3 kg and is breathing deeply at rest. He has not previously been diagnosed with diabetes. He is alert and oriented, capillary refill is three seconds and mucous membranes are dry. His weight today is 25 kg.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.0 °C, HR 132, BP 102/64, RR 30, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Deep Kussmaul breathing, acetone breath, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, GCS 15, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: BSL 26 mmol/L, capillary ketones 5.2 mmol/L, venous pH 7.18, HCO3 9 mmol/L, Na 132 mmol/L, K 4.4 mmol/L, urea 9.1 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, corrected sodium 136 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate fluid prescription for the first hour?

options.text: 10 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 60 minutes followed by deficit plus maintenance calculated over 48 hours with potassium added
options.rationale: Australian paediatric DKA guidelines use a single 10 mL/kg bolus followed by deficit replacement over 48 hours; this approach minimises rapid osmolar shifts that precipitate cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: 20 mL/kg 0.9% sodium chloride bolus over 20 minutes repeated until perfusion improves followed by maintenance fluids at twice the normal rate
options.rationale: Large repeated boluses and accelerated maintenance increase the risk of cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA; current Australian practice restricts bolus to 10 mL/kg unless shocked. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Commence intravenous 8.4% sodium bicarbonate infusion at 2 mmol/kg over the first hour to correct the acidosis before fluids and insulin
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not recommended in paediatric DKA because it worsens intracellular acidosis and increases cerebral oedema risk without changing outcome. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Start intravenous insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately and withhold all crystalloid fluids until the blood glucose has fallen below 14 mmol/L
options.rationale: Insulin without preceding fluid resuscitation worsens dehydration and precipitates hypokalaemia; fluid is started first and insulin is added after the first hour. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Give 5% dextrose in 0.45% sodium chloride at twice maintenance rate immediately to provide glucose substrate and prevent further ketogenesis before insulin
options.rationale: Dextrose-containing fluid is added later when glucose falls below 14 mmol/L; initial resuscitation uses isotonic crystalloid to restore intravascular volume. (Source: APEG Paediatric DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is the leading cause of death in paediatric DKA and is linked to rapid fluid shifts. Australian APEG and RCH guidelines therefore standardise resuscitation to a single 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus unless the child is in shock, followed by calculated deficit replacement spread over 48 hours and maintenance using a two-bag system with potassium. Insulin infusion at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/hour begins after the first hour of fluids. Bicarbonate, large repeated boluses and accelerated correction are explicitly avoided.
key_points: Initial bolus is 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline unless shocked
Deficit is replaced over 48 hours, not 24
Insulin starts at least one hour after fluid resuscitation begins
Bicarbonate is not used in paediatric DKA
Cerebral oedema risk peaks 4 to 12 hours after treatment start
explanation.pearls: A sudden headache, altered conscious state or bradycardia after starting therapy mandates immediate 3% saline 5 mL/kg bolus and reduction of fluid rate for suspected cerebral oedema.

subtopic_detail: Unexplained bruising in a non-mobile infant
stem: You are the paediatric resident reviewing a 5-month-old non-Aboriginal infant brought to the emergency department by her mother for unsettled crying. She is not yet rolling. While undressing her you notice two purple bruises over the left mandible and one over the right pinna. Her mother says she has not noticed them and gives no clear mechanism. The child is otherwise alert and feeding.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 °C, HR 132, RR 36, SpO2 100% room air
data_table.Examination: Two 1 cm purple bruises over left mandible, one over right pinna, anterior fontanelle soft, no torn frenulum, no skeletal tenderness, normal neurological exam
data_table.Investigations: FBE, coagulation studies, LFTs and lipase pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Admit for skeletal survey, head CT, ophthalmology review for retinal haemorrhages, and immediate child protection notification to the relevant state authority
options.rationale: Bruising in a non-mobile infant or in protected sites such as ears and mandible is a sentinel injury and requires admission, full non-accidental injury workup and statutory child protection notification. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Discharge home with safety netting advice once coagulation studies return normal, and arrange general practitioner review in 48 hours for ongoing assessment
options.rationale: Normal coagulation does not exclude inflicted injury; the location and mechanism profile mandate workup and notification regardless of laboratory results. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Reassure the mother that bruising in this distribution is common in infants and arrange paediatric outpatient review within one week for reassessment
options.rationale: Bruising in non-mobile infants is rare and never normal; reassurance is unsafe and misses a sentinel injury that predicts escalation. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

options.text: Request a urine drug screen and a metabolic bone disease panel to exclude inherited causes before notifying any external agencies of suspected harm
options.rationale: Delaying notification while awaiting metabolic workup is not safe; mandatory reporting is triggered by suspicion alone and proceeds in parallel with investigation. (Source: RACGP Child Protection Resource)

options.text: Refer to the social work team for community follow-up only and arrange return for repeat examination in two weeks if any further bruises appear at home
options.rationale: Community follow-up alone for an infant with a sentinel injury risks serious further harm; statutory notification and acute admission are required. (Source: RCH CPG: Child Abuse)

explanation.summary: Bruising in a non-mobile infant, or bruising in protected sites such as ears, mandible, neck, torso, buttocks or genitalia at any age, is a sentinel injury for non-accidental injury. The rule "those who don't cruise rarely bruise" highlights how rare any bruise is before independent mobility. Investigation includes skeletal survey, neuroimaging, retinal exam, coagulation studies and consideration of metabolic bone disease. Statutory mandatory reporting to child protection is triggered by suspicion and is independent of investigation results.
key_points: Any bruise in a non-mobile infant is concerning until proven otherwise
Bruising of ears, mandible, neck and torso is high specificity for inflicted injury
Skeletal survey plus neuroimaging plus retinal review forms the standard workup
Mandatory reporting is triggered by suspicion, not confirmation
A normal coagulation panel does not exclude inflicted injury
explanation.pearls: Up to 30% of children later confirmed as physically abused have a recognised sentinel injury in the preceding weeks that was not reported, so escalation of any single suspicious bruise is the inflection point for prevention.

stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-week-old non-Aboriginal infant brought in by his parents for tactile fever and reduced feeding for 12 hours. He was born at term, breastfed and previously well. He has no rash and no cough. His mother had a negative group B Streptococcus screen at 36 weeks and labour was uncomplicated. He has not received any antibiotics.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4 °C rectal, HR 168, RR 52, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Pink, perfused, anterior fontanelle soft and flat, no rash, normal tone, no focus of infection identified on full undressed examination
data_table.Investigations: Pending - FBE, blood culture, urine via catheter, lumbar puncture, CRP, viral PCR

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home on oral cefalexin 25 mg/kg three times daily with general practice review in 24 hours and clear advice about returning for warning signs
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics in a febrile infant under 3 months are inadequate cover for occult bacteraemia or meningitis and the infant requires admission with parenteral therapy and full workup. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Admit for full septic screen including lumbar puncture and commence intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin pending culture results
options.rationale: Australian guidelines mandate full sepsis evaluation including lumbar puncture and empiric intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin for any febrile infant under 3 months without a clear viral focus. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Perform urinalysis and chest x-ray only, and observe for four hours in the short stay unit before deciding on antibiotics if no source is identified
options.rationale: A limited workup misses occult meningitis and bacteraemia in this age group; the full septic screen plus empiric antibiotics is the standard of care under 3 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Admit for observation without antibiotics until blood culture or urine culture results are available, given that the infant currently looks well clinically
options.rationale: Delaying empiric antibiotics in a young febrile infant is unsafe because clinical appearance is a poor predictor of serious bacterial infection at this age. (Source: RCH CPG: Febrile Child)

options.text: Start intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg monotherapy and admit for observation without performing a lumbar puncture in a well-appearing infant
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone alone misses Listeria cover required under 3 months, and lumbar puncture is part of the standard workup in this age group regardless of clinical appearance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Febrile infants under 90 days have a significantly higher rate of serious bacterial infection than older children and clinical appearance is unreliable. Australian guidelines mandate a full septic screen including urine collected by catheter or supra-pubic aspirate, blood culture, lumbar puncture, FBE, CRP and consideration of viral PCR. Empiric intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin covers Listeria, group B Streptococcus and E coli, the most common pathogens at this age. Cefotaxime is added if meningitis is suspected.
key_points: Under 90 days, clinical appearance does not exclude serious bacterial infection
Full septic workup includes blood culture, urine, and lumbar puncture
First-line empiric therapy is intravenous benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin
Cefotaxime replaces ceftriaxone in neonates because of bilirubin displacement
Viral PCR may shorten antibiotic course if positive and infant clinically improves
explanation.pearls: Cefotaxime is preferred over ceftriaxone in the first 28 days of life because ceftriaxone displaces bilirubin from albumin and risks kernicterus.

subtopic_detail: Nausea and vomiting of pregnancy first-line
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 28-year-old G1P0 at 9 weeks gestation by her last menstrual period with three weeks of daily nausea and intermittent vomiting up to four times daily. She has lost 1 kg from her booking weight but is tolerating small sips of fluid. Her mid-stream urine ketone strip is negative. Her dating ultrasound shows a single viable intrauterine pregnancy. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6 °C, HR 84, BP 112/72, RR 16, weight 64 kg (booking 65 kg)
data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes mildly dry, abdomen soft and non-tender, no thyroid enlargement
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: ketones negative, specific gravity 1.020; beta-hCG appropriate for dates; TSH within normal range

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily combined with oral doxylamine 12.5 mg at night and small frequent low-fat meals with antiemetic review in one week
options.rationale: Pyridoxine plus doxylamine is the first-line combination for nausea and vomiting in pregnancy in Australian and international guidelines because it has the best safety evidence and is effective for mild to moderate symptoms. (Source: SOMANZ Hyperemesis Guideline)

options.text: Oral ondansetron 8 mg three times daily as first-line monotherapy with a follow-up appointment arranged in one week to assess response and side effects
options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for failure of first-line measures because of cleft palate signal in some first-trimester studies and constipation risk; pyridoxine and doxylamine are preferred initially. (Source: SOMANZ Hyperemesis Guideline)

options.text: Admit for intravenous Hartmann's fluid resuscitation, thiamine cover and intravenous metoclopramide pending resolution of symptoms before discharge
options.rationale: Admission with intravenous therapy is reserved for hyperemesis gravidarum with ketonuria, weight loss over 5%, dehydration or electrolyte disturbance, not for mild outpatient nausea. (Source: SOMANZ Hyperemesis Guideline)

options.text: Reassure her that nausea will settle by 12 weeks and provide written advice on ginger tea and acupressure bands without any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Symptoms troubling enough to cause weight loss warrant pharmacological treatment to prevent progression to hyperemesis; lifestyle advice alone is insufficient at this point. (Source: SOMANZ Hyperemesis Guideline)

options.text: Oral promethazine 25 mg every six hours and arrange a thyroid-stimulating hormone level today to exclude hyperthyroidism as a contributing differential cause
options.rationale: Promethazine is a second-line agent and routine thyroid testing is unnecessary in mild symptoms with a TSH already documented as normal in the stem. (Source: SOMANZ Hyperemesis Guideline)

explanation.summary: Nausea and vomiting affect up to 80% of pregnancies and a smaller proportion progress to hyperemesis gravidarum. SOMANZ recommends a stepped approach: lifestyle measures, then pyridoxine plus doxylamine, then metoclopramide or promethazine, with ondansetron reserved for resistant cases. Indications for admission include ketonuria, weight loss above 5%, electrolyte disturbance or inability to tolerate any oral intake. Persistent severe symptoms warrant thyroid function, urinalysis and consideration of molar pregnancy.
key_points: First-line is pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily plus doxylamine 12.5 mg nocte
Ondansetron is later line because of first-trimester cleft palate signal and constipation
Admission triggered by ketonuria, weight loss over 5% or electrolyte disturbance
Thiamine 100 mg cover for any prolonged vomiting before glucose-containing fluids
Persistent severe symptoms prompt workup for molar pregnancy and thyroid dysfunction
explanation.pearls: Thiamine 100 mg intravenous or oral is given before any dextrose-containing fluid in prolonged hyperemesis to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.

stem: You are the general practice obstetric shared-care registrar reviewing a 32-year-old G2P1 at 30 weeks gestation who is asking when her swab to look for the bacteria that causes early newborn infection should be done. Her previous baby was term, healthy and did not develop early-onset sepsis. She has had no urinary tract infections this pregnancy. Her booking ultrasound was unremarkable and she is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Fundal height appropriate for dates, fetal heart heard, no uterine activity
data_table.Investigations: Antenatal bloods normal, 28 week OGTT normal, current MSU pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding screening timing?

options.text: Combined vaginal and rectal swab is recommended at 35 to 37 weeks gestation, with intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis offered to those who screen positive
options.rationale: Australian RANZCOG guidance recommends universal vaginal-rectal swab screening at 35 to 37 weeks because culture remains valid for around 5 weeks and predicts colonisation status at delivery. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement)

options.text: Vaginal swab alone is recommended at 28 weeks gestation, with repeat testing at 36 weeks if the first sample returns positive on initial culture
options.rationale: Rectal sampling is essential for sensitivity and 28 weeks is too early; recolonisation between sampling and birth limits the predictive value of earlier swabs. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement)

options.text: Mid-stream urine culture alone is sufficient for screening, with vaginal swab reserved only for those whose urine sample returns culture positive for GBS
options.rationale: Urine culture detects only heavy colonisation and misses most carriers; it does not replace vaginal-rectal swabbing in the universal screening pathway. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement)

options.text: Screening is unnecessary because her previous baby did not have early-onset GBS sepsis, and intrapartum antibiotics are reserved for risk-based protocols
options.rationale: Australia uses universal culture-based screening rather than a risk-based approach; previous well-baby status does not waive current pregnancy screening because colonisation varies. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement)

options.text: Combined vaginal and rectal swab is recommended at 40 weeks gestation, immediately before the onset of labour, so that the result reflects colonisation at birth
options.rationale: Sampling at 40 weeks is too close to delivery for culture turnaround; 35 to 37 weeks balances result validity with time to inform intrapartum decisions. (Source: RANZCOG GBS Statement)

explanation.summary: Early-onset group B Streptococcus disease is the leading cause of neonatal sepsis. Australian practice has shifted to universal culture-based screening with a combined vaginal-rectal swab at 35 to 37 weeks gestation, because culture results remain predictive of intrapartum colonisation for roughly five weeks. Women who screen positive, who have GBS bacteriuria in the current pregnancy, or who have a previous baby with invasive GBS disease are offered intrapartum benzylpenicillin. The risk-based approach is retained for unbooked women or those with unknown status at the onset of labour with risk factors.
key_points: Swab at 35 to 37 weeks gestation, combined vaginal and rectal
Universal screening rather than risk-based is the current Australian standard
Intrapartum benzylpenicillin 3 g loading then 1.8 g every 4 hours is first line
GBS bacteriuria in current pregnancy is an automatic prophylaxis indication
Risk-based approach applies if status unknown at labour onset
explanation.pearls: GBS bacteriuria at any threshold during the current pregnancy warrants intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis and treatment of the urinary tract infection at the time of detection.
